primary_diagnosis.name: Seronegative inflammatory (rheumatoid) arthritis flare, precipitated by NSAID cessation

pathophysiology_summary: Seronegative inflammatory arthritis causes symmetric small-joint synovitis with prolonged morning stiffness and systemic fatigue. Withdrawal of the usual NSAID allowed a severe flare with bilateral wrist/MCP/PIP synovitis and functional loss of grip. This mimics infectious tenosynovitis/cellulitis and a septic joint, but bilateral symmetric involvement, absence of a single hot/red joint or tracking erythema, and only low-grade fever point to an inflammatory flare. Telehealth cannot examine the joints, but re-issuing the patient's usual (and historically effective) NSAID that was stopped is an appropriate remote step, alongside septic-arthritis/cellulitis safety-netting, a prompt in-person joint review and rheumatology work-up, and practical support given she cannot open medication or prepare food while living alone. Urgent care is warranted for any single fiery joint, tracking erythema, or high fever/rigors.

differential.evidence_basis: Symmetric synovitis with prolonged stiffness after stopping the NSAID.
Screen for tracking erythema/single tendon sheath.
Less likely given symmetric MCP synovitis and prolonged stiffness.
No single hot joint/tracking erythema, low fever, clear NSAID-cessation trigger.
Low probability but not excludable without an exam.
Working telehealth diagnosis: restart the usual NSAID, safety-net septic arthritis/cellulitis, arrange in-person joint review and rheumatology work-up, and provide practical support.

red_flags.description: A single joint becoming fiery red/hugely swollen, tracking erythema up the arm, or high fever with rigors
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate septic arthritis or spreading soft-tissue infection needing urgent care.

red_flags.description: Functional loss of hand use with inability to take medication/eat while living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Risks non-administration of medication, poor intake and unsafe self-care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed medication, dehydration and functional deterioration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Re-issuing the NSAID without screening for a septic joint/cellulitis or arranging a joint review.
Ignoring that she cannot open medication or prepare food while isolated.
Attempting an IM NSAID over chat.
Not arranging rheumatology referral for ongoing seronegative RA management.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: No single septic-looking joint, no tracking erythema, low fever — low septic probability
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports restarting the NSAID with safety-netting and prompt joint review.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Joint examination for synovitis distribution, erythema and tracking infection
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Re-issue the usual NSAID, screen septic arthritis/cellulitis, arrange a prompt in-person joint review and rheumatology work-up, and organise practical support (opening medication, meals); escalate urgently for a single fiery joint, tracking erythema, or high fever/rigors.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Re-issuing an NSAID without safety-netting could miss a septic joint/cellulitis; ignoring the self-care needs risks medication non-administration and dehydration.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Rheumatology (RA flare/acute hot joint)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Inflammatory arthritis flares are managed with NSAIDs/steroids and DMARD optimisation; a single acutely inflamed joint requires aspiration to exclude sepsis.
Early rheumatology referral and DMARD therapy are recommended for persistent inflammatory arthritis.
